title: Maximum Score of a Good Subarray
description: Find maximum score of subarray containing index k

examples.explanation: n=6, k=3. Initialize max_score=0. i=0: j=3: subarray [1,4,3,7], min=1, length=4, score=4, max_score=4. j=4: subarray [1,4,3,7,4], min=1, length=5, score=5, max_score=5. j=5: subarray [1,4,3,7,4,5], min=1, length=6, score=6, max_score=6. i=1: j=3: [4,3,7], min=3, length=3, score=9, max_score=9. j=4: [4,3,7,4], min=3, length=4, score=12, max_score=12. j=5: [4,3,7,4,5], min=3, length=5, score=15, max_score=15. i=2: j=3: [3,7], min=3, length=2, score=6, max_score=15. j=4: [3,7,4], min=3, length=3, score=9, max_score=15. j=5: [3,7,4,5], min=3, length=4, score=12, max_score=15. i=3: j=3: [7], min=7, length=1, score=7, max_score=15. j=4: [7,4], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=15. j=5: [7,4,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=15. Return 15.

n=5, k=2. max_score=0. i=0: j=2: [5,5,4], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=12. j=3: [5,5,4,5], min=4, length=4, score=16, max_score=16. j=4: [5,5,4,5,5], min=4, length=5, score=20, max_score=20. i=1: j=2: [5,4], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=20. j=3: [5,4,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=20. j=4: [5,4,5,5], min=4, length=4, score=16, max_score=20. i=2: j=2: [4], min=4, length=1, score=4, max_score=20. j=3: [4,5], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=20. j=4: [4,5,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=20. Return 20.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all possible subarrays that include index k, compute the minimum element in each subarray multiplied by its length, and track the maximum score. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Score of Good Subarray
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution to find maximum score of subarray containing index k

explanation.prose: The brute force approach solves the problem by systematically examining all possible subarrays that include the given index k, calculating their scores, and tracking the maximum. The intuition is straightforward: since we need subarrays containing k, we can consider all possible left boundaries (i from 0 to k) and right boundaries (j from k to n-1). For each pair (i,j), we compute the minimum element in nums[i.j] and multiply it by the subarray length (j-i+1) to get the score. The maximum score across all such subarrays is our answer. This works because it exhaustively checks every valid subarray, guaranteeing correctness.

connectsTo.reason: by using the subarray enumeration as the key subroutine

timeComplexity.note: due to nested loops and O(n) min computation in worst case, though we can optimize min tracking to O(1) per iteration by maintaining running minimum

commonMistakes: forgetting to include k in subarrays (must have i ≤ k ≤ j)
incorrectly computing length (should be j-i+1)
inefficient min computation (should track running minimum)
explanation.codePattern: def maxScore(nums, k): n=len(nums); max_score=0; for i in range(k+1): min_val=nums[i]; for j in range(k, n): if j>k: min_val=min(min_val, nums[j]); for left in range(i, k): min_val=min(min_val, nums[left]); score=min_val*(j-i+1); max_score=max(max_score, score); return max_score
algorithm: 2. For i from 0 to k: For j from k to n-1: Compute min_val = minimum of nums[i..j]. Compute score = min_val * (j-i+1). Update max_score = max(max_score, score)

examples.trace: n=6, k=3. Initialize max_score=0. i=0: j=3: subarray [1,4,3,7], min=1, length=4, score=4, max_score=4. j=4: subarray [1,4,3,7,4], min=1, length=5, score=5, max_score=5. j=5: subarray [1,4,3,7,4,5], min=1, length=6, score=6, max_score=6. i=1: j=3: [4,3,7], min=3, length=3, score=9, max_score=9. j=4: [4,3,7,4], min=3, length=4, score=12, max_score=12. j=5: [4,3,7,4,5], min=3, length=5, score=15, max_score=15. i=2: j=3: [3,7], min=3, length=2, score=6, max_score=15. j=4: [3,7,4], min=3, length=3, score=9, max_score=15. j=5: [3,7,4,5], min=3, length=4, score=12, max_score=15. i=3: j=3: [7], min=7, length=1, score=7, max_score=15. j=4: [7,4], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=15. j=5: [7,4,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=15. Return 15.

n=5, k=2. max_score=0. i=0: j=2: [5,5,4], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=12. j=3: [5,5,4,5], min=4, length=4, score=16, max_score=16. j=4: [5,5,4,5,5], min=4, length=5, score=20, max_score=20. i=1: j=2: [5,4], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=20. j=3: [5,4,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=20. j=4: [5,4,5,5], min=4, length=4, score=16, max_score=20. i=2: j=2: [4], min=4, length=1, score=4, max_score=20. j=3: [4,5], min=4, length=2, score=8, max_score=20. j=4: [4,5,5], min=4, length=3, score=12, max_score=20. Return 20.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Enumerate All Subarrays Containing Index k
ladders.desc: Generate all possible subarrays that include a specific index k

explanation.prose: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for generating every subarray that contains the given index k. The intuition is that any subarray containing k must have its left boundary i at or before k (0 ≤ i ≤ k) and its right boundary j at or after k (k ≤ j ≤ n-1). By iterating over all possible i and j within these ranges, we cover all valid subarrays. This ensures we don't miss any candidate subarray while avoiding invalid ones that exclude k.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the subarrays for score computation

timeComplexity.note: for generation alone, and space is O(1)

commonMistakes: using wrong bounds (e.g., i from 0 to n-1 without ensuring i ≤ k ≤ j)
off-by-one errors
generating duplicate subarrays (not an issue here)
explanation.codePattern: for i in range(k+1): for j in range(k, n): subarray = nums[i:j+1]. The step-by-step: For each i, j expands rightward from k, ensuring k is always included. This systematic expansion guarantees all subarrays are considered exactly once
algorithm: 2. For i from 0 to k: For j from k to n-1: Process subarray nums[i..j]. This creates O(n²) subarrays in worst case.

examples.trace: n=2, k=0. i=0: j=0: [5]. j=1: [5,6]. Total 2 subarrays.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Score as Minimum × Length
ladders.desc: Calculate score for a subarray by multiplying its minimum element by its length

explanation.prose: This core logic defines how to compute the score for any given subarray, which is fundamental to solving the problem. The intuition is based on the problem definition: the score of a subarray is the minimum value in that subarray multiplied by the subarray's length. This captures the trade-off between how small the minimum is and how large the subarray is. A larger subarray with a moderately small minimum might score higher than a smaller subarray with a very small minimum. The algorithm: Given a subarray nums[i.j], 1. Find min_val = minimum element in nums[i.j]. 2. Compute length = j - i + 1. 3. Compute score = min_val * length. This is straightforward but requires efficient min computation, especially when integrated into loops. Time complexity for a single subarray is O(length) for naive min finding, but can be optimized.

connectsTo.reason: by applying this computation to each generated subarray

commonMistakes: using wrong length formula (e.g., j-i instead of j-i+1)
incorrectly computing min (e.g., missing elements)
integer overflow (not typical in this problem)
explanation.codePattern: min_val = min(nums[i:j+1]); length = j-i+1; score = min_val * length

examples.trace: Elements: 3,7,4. min_val = min(3,7,4) = 3. length = 2-0+1 = 3. score = 3 * 3 = 9.
Assume i=1,j=3 in original context, but for subarray [5,5,5], min_val = 5. length = 3. score = 5 * 3 = 15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Minimum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Compute the minimum element within a contiguous subarray of an array

explanation.prose: This building block is essential for computing the score, as it finds the smallest value in a given subarray. The intuition is simple: traverse the subarray and keep track of the smallest element seen so far. This is a fundamental array operation. The algorithm: Given array nums and indices i and j (inclusive), 1. Initialize min_val = nums[i]. 2. For index from i+1 to j: Update min_val = min(min_val, nums[index]). 3. Return min_val.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the min_val for score calculation

timeComplexity.note: where length = j-i+1, and space is O(1)

commonMistakes: starting loop from i instead of i+1 (redundant)
using wrong indices (e.g., exceeding array bounds)
not handling empty subarrays (not applicable here as i ≤ j)
explanation.codePattern: min_val = nums[i]; for idx in range(i+1, j+1): min_val = min(min_val, nums[idx])

examples.trace: min_val = nums[0] = 5. idx=1: nums[1]=3, min(5,3)=3. idx=2: nums[2]=8, min(3,8)=3. idx=3: nums[3]=1, min(3,1)=1. Return 1.
min_val = nums[1] = 4. idx=2: nums[2]=4, min(4,4)=4. Return 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through a range of indices in an array to access elements

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves using loops to traverse specific sections of an array, which is fundamental to many algorithms including our brute force approach. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, and we often need to process elements sequentially by their indices. This allows us to perform operations like finding minima, computing sums, or generating subarrays. The algorithm: For a given start index i and end index j (inclusive), use a loop to go from i to j. In pseudocode: for index in range(i, j+1): access nums[index].

connectsTo.reason: by providing the mechanism to traverse a subarray for min finding

timeComplexity.note: for the loop, and space is O(1)

commonMistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(i,j) which excludes j)
incorrect bounds leading to index errors
misunderstanding zero-based indexing
explanation.codePattern: for idx in range(i, j+1): val = nums[idx]. The step-by-step: Initialize idx = i, check if idx ≤ j, execute loop body, increment idx, repeat until idx > j. This ensures each element in the range is visited exactly once

examples.trace: idx=0: nums[0]=10. idx=1: nums[1]=20. idx=2: nums[2]=30. Loop ends.
idx=1: nums[1]=6. idx=2: nums[2]=7. idx=3: nums[3]=8. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value at a specific position in an array using its index

explanation.prose: This is the most fundamental concept required for the problem: understanding how to access individual elements in an array. Arrays are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, each identified by an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that by knowing an index, we can directly retrieve the value stored at that position, which is a constant-time operation. This is crucial for any algorithm that processes arrays. The operation: Given an array nums and an index i, access nums[i] to get the value.

connectsTo.reason: as the basic action performed during iteration

commonMistakes: using 1-based indexing (e.g., nums[1] for first element)
accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., i ≥ length of array)
confusing indices with values

examples.trace: Array has indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Access index 1: nums[1] = 10.
Indices: 0->100, 1->200. Access index 0: nums[0] = 100.

approaches.name: Two Pointer Expansion O(n)
approaches.summary: Start with subarray [k, k] and expand left and right pointers outward, always moving the pointer from the side with the larger adjacent value to maximize the minimum element. This greedy approach efficiently finds the optimal subarray.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Score of a Good Subarray Using Two Pointer Expansion
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum score of subarray containing index k using greedy two-pointer expansion.

explanation.prose: The problem requires finding a subarray containing index k that maximizes the score, defined as (minimum element in subarray) × (length of subarray). The key insight is that we can start with the subarray [k, k] and expand outward greedily, always moving the pointer from the side with the larger adjacent value to maximize the minimum element. This works because the score depends on both the minimum value and the length. By expanding toward larger values, we maintain a higher minimum while increasing length. The algorithm initializes left = right = k, min_val = nums[k], and max_score = min_val. While we can expand left (left > 0) or right (right < n-1), we compare nums[left-1] and nums[right+1]. If nums[left-1] > nums[right+1], we expand left: left -= 1, update min_val = min(min_val, nums[left]), else expand right: right += 1, update min_val = min(min_val, nums[right]). After each expansion, update max_score = max(max_score, min_val × (right-left+1)). Continue until both pointers reach boundaries. This greedy approach is optimal because at each step, choosing the larger adjacent value gives the best chance to maintain a high minimum.

connectsTo.reason: This uses the expansion logic as the core subroutine

timeComplexity.note: as each pointer moves at most n times

commonMistakes: not updating min_val correctly
stopping expansion prematurely
explanation.codePattern: Initialize pointers and min_val, loop while expansion possible, compare adjacent values, expand, update min_val and max_score, return max_score

examples.trace: Start: left=3, right=3, min_val=7, max_score=7×1=7. Expand: left-1=2 (3), right+1=4 (4). Choose right (4>3): right=4, min_val=min(7,4)=4, max_score=max(7,4×2=8)=8. Expand: left-1=2 (3), right+1=5 (5). Choose right (5>3): right=5, min_val=min(4,5)=4, max_score=max(8,4×3=12)=12. Expand: left-1=2 (3), right at end. Choose left: left=2, min_val=min(4,3)=3, max_score=max(12,3×4=12)=12. Expand: left-1=1 (4), right at end. Choose left: left=1, min_val=min(3,4)=3, max_score=max(12,3×5=15)=15. Expand: left-1=0 (1), right at end. Choose left: left=0, min_val=min(3,1)=1, max_score=max(15,1×6=6)=15. Stop: max_score=15.

Start: left=2, right=2, min_val=2, max_score=2×1=2. Expand: left-1=1 (3), right+1=3 (4). Choose right (4>3): right=3, min_val=min(2,4)=2, max_score=max(2,2×2=4)=4. Expand: left-1=1 (3), right+1=4 (1). Choose left (3>1): left=1, min_val=min(2,3)=2, max_score=max(4,2×3=6)=6. Expand: left-1=0 (5), right+1=4 (1). Choose left (5>1): left=0, min_val=min(2,5)=2, max_score=max(6,2×4=8)=8. Expand: left at start, right+1=4 (1). Choose right: right=4, min_val=min(2,1)=1, max_score=max(8,1×5=5)=8. Stop: max_score=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy Two-Pointer Expansion Logic
ladders.desc: Implement the expansion logic that decides which pointer to move based on adjacent values.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the heart of the algorithm. Given an array, current left and right pointers, and current minimum value, it decides whether to expand left or right. The decision is greedy: compare the value to the left of left (if exists) and to the right of right (if exists). Choose to expand toward the larger value because this maximizes the potential minimum in the expanded subarray. If left cannot expand (left=0), expand right; if right cannot expand (right=n-1), expand left. After expansion, update the minimum value by taking min(current min, new element). This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main algorithm. Intuition: Expanding toward larger values helps maintain a higher minimum, which is crucial for the score formula (min × length). Step-by-step: Check if left>0 and right<n-1. If both, compare nums[left-1] and nums[right+1]. If nums[left-1] > nums[right+1], expand left: left -= 1, update min_val = min(min_val, nums[left]). Else expand right: right += 1, update min_val = min(min_val, nums[right]). If only one side can expand, expand that side. Time O(1) per call, space O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This is the core operation in the main loop

commonMistakes: Forgetting to handle cases where one pointer cannot expand
explanation.codePattern: Function expand(left, right, min_val, nums): if left>0 and right<len(nums)-1: if nums[left-1] > nums[right+1]: left -= 1, min_val = min(min_val, nums[left]); else: right += 1, min_val = min(min_val, nums[right]); elif left>0: left -= 1, min_val = min(min_val, nums[left]); else: right += 1, min_val = min(min_val, nums[right]); return left, right, min_val

examples.trace: Current: left=1, right=1, min_val=7. Check expansion: left-1=0 (3), right+1=2 (2). Compare: 3 > 2? Yes. Expand left: left=0, min_val=min(7,3)=3. Return left=0, right=1, min_val=3.
Current: left=2, right=2, min_val=6. Check expansion: left-1=1 (1), right+1=3 (3). Compare: 1 > 3? No. Expand right: right=3, min_val=min(6,3)=3. Return left=2, right=3, min_val=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Choice for Maximizing Minimum in Expanding Subarray
ladders.desc: Understand why expanding toward larger adjacent values maximizes the minimum element.

explanation.prose: The core insight is that to maximize the score (min × length), we need to balance increasing length with maintaining a high minimum. When expanding a subarray, we have two choices: expand left or right. The greedy choice is to expand toward the larger adjacent value because this gives the best chance to keep the minimum high. Consider current subarray with minimum m. If we expand to include a value x, the new minimum becomes min(m, x). To maximize this new minimum, we want x to be as large as possible. Therefore, at each step, compare the left adjacent value L and right adjacent value R. If L > R, expanding left gives min(m, L) which is at least as good as min(m, R). This greedy approach is optimal for this problem because the score depends monotonically on both min and length; there's no benefit to choosing a smaller value early. Intuition: Think of the minimum as a limiting factor. By always picking the larger adjacent value, we delay reducing the minimum, allowing length to increase more before the minimum drops. This is a local optimal choice that leads to global optimum. Step-by-step: At any state, evaluate L and R. Choose max(L, R). Update min = min(current min, chosen value). This logic is applied repeatedly. Time O(1) per decision, space O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This logic is implemented in the expansion subroutine

commonMistakes: Not realizing that this greedy choice is optimal for this specific problem
explanation.codePattern: if L > R: expand left, else expand right

examples.trace: Given min=4, left adjacent L=7, right adjacent R=3. Compare L and R: 7 > 3. Choose to expand left because including 7 gives new min = min(4,7)=4, while expanding right gives new min = min(4,3)=3. Expanding left keeps min higher at 4.

Given min=2, L=1, R=5. Compare L and R: 1 > 5? No. Choose to expand right because including 5 gives new min = min(2,5)=2, while expanding left gives new min = min(2,1)=1. Expanding right keeps min higher at 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Maintaining Minimum Value in a Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Learn how to keep track of the minimum element in a dynamically expanding subarray.

explanation.prose: This building block focuses on efficiently maintaining the minimum value in a subarray as it expands. When we expand a subarray by adding one element, the new minimum is simply the minimum of the old minimum and the new element. This is a constant-time update. Intuition: The minimum of a set of numbers is the smallest value among them. When adding a new number x to a subarray with current minimum m, the new minimum is min(m, x). This is because if x < m, then x becomes the new smallest; otherwise, m remains the smallest. This property allows us to update the minimum in O(1) time without scanning the entire subarray. Step-by-step: Start with initial min_val = value at starting index. Each time we add a new element at index i, update min_val = min(min_val, nums[i]). This ensures min_val always holds the minimum of the current subarray. Time O(1) per update, space O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This update is used after each greedy expansion to keep the minimum current

commonMistakes: Forgetting to update min_val
incorrectly updating it (e.g., using max instead of min)

examples.input: Current subarray values: [5,3,7], min_val=3. Add new element: 2
examples.trace: Current min_val is 3 (minimum of [5,3,7]). Add new element 2. Update min_val = min(3, 2) = 2. New subarray is [5,3,7,2], minimum is 2.

examples.input: Current subarray values: [1,4,2], min_val=1. Add new element: 5
examples.trace: Current min_val is 1 (minimum of [1,4,2]). Add new element 5. Update min_val = min(1, 5) = 1. New subarray is [1,4,2,5], minimum remains 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Adjacent Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: Compare elements to the immediate left and right of given indices.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves accessing and comparing elements in an array that are adjacent to current positions. Given an array nums, a left index l, and a right index r, we need to compare nums[l-1] (if l>0) and nums[r+1] (if r<len(nums)-1). This is fundamental for deciding which direction to expand. Intuition: In array traversal, we often need to look at neighbors. Here, we compare the left neighbor and right neighbor to make a decision. Step-by-step: Check if l > 0 to ensure left neighbor exists; if so, get left_val = nums[l-1]. Check if r < n-1 to ensure right neighbor exists; if so, get right_val = nums[r+1]. Then compare left_val and right_val. Time O(1), space O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This comparison is used to decide which new element to add when updating the minimum

commonMistakes: Accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., l-1 when l=0)
explanation.codePattern: left_val = nums[l-1] if l > 0 else None; right_val = nums[r+1] if r < n-1 else None; then compare if both exist

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:2, 1:5, 2:1, 3:8. l=1, so left neighbor at index l-1=0: value 2. r=2, so right neighbor at index r+1=3: value 8. Compare: 2 and 8.
l=0, so no left neighbor (l-1=-1 invalid). r=1, right neighbor at index r+1=2: value 4. Only right_val exists.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subarrays and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what a subarray is and how to access elements in an array using indices.

explanation.prose: A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements within an array. For example, in array [1,2,3,4], subarrays include [1], [1,2], [2,3,4], etc. It is defined by a start index and an end index, inclusive. Array indexing is how we access individual elements: in most programming languages, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. To access an element at index i, we use syntax like nums[i]. To define a subarray from index l to r, we include all elements from nums[l] to nums[r]. Intuition: Think of an array as a list of boxes numbered from 0. A subarray is a range of consecutive boxes. This foundation is crucial because our problem involves expanding a subarray by moving left and right pointers. Step-by-step: Given array of size n, indices range from 0 to n-1. Subarray length = r-l+1. To expand left, decrease l; to expand right, increase r. Time O(1) to access elements, space O(1) for pointers.

connectsTo.reason: This basic knowledge is needed to compare adjacent elements

commonMistakes: Off-by-one errors
confusing length with indices
explanation.codePattern: l=start_index, r=start_index; to expand: l -= 1 or r += 1

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40. l=1, r=2. Subarray includes elements at indices 1 and 2: 20 and 30. So subarray is [20,30]. Length = r-l+1 = 2-1+1 = 2.
l=0, r=0. Subarray includes only element at index 0: 5. So subarray is [5]. Length = 0-0+1 = 1.
